content.text: TypeScript builds on JavaScript's variable declarations —

— and adds a type annotation layer on top. Understanding the scoping and hoisting rules of each keyword, and when TypeScript infers vs. requires an explicit type, is the foundation for writing safe Angular code.

attrs.alt: var vs let vs const — scope, hoisting, and re-assignment comparison

TypeScript lets you annotate a variable's type after a colon. The annotation constrains what values can be assigned and what operations are legal on the variable.
Type annotations are optional when TypeScript can infer the type from the initial value. They are most useful when the initial value is
, or an empty collection, since inference would produce an undesirably narrow type like

When you initialise a variable at the point of declaration, TypeScript infers its type from the value. You do not need to annotate it explicitly — the inferred type is just as enforced as an explicit annotation.
Relying on inference for local variables keeps code readable without sacrificing type safety. Reserve explicit annotations for function parameters, return types, and class properties.
Value that might not yet be set
Value from an external source (API, user input)
Value that can never exist (exhaustive checks)
Array that should not be mutated
when a type is genuinely unknown at compile time.
forces you to narrow the type before using it;
silently opts the variable out of type checking entirely.
TypeScript supports all JavaScript destructuring syntax, and types can be annotated or inferred in destructuring expressions.
modifier prevents property re-assignment on objects and arrays at the TypeScript level. For deep immutability, use the
In Angular, use `as const` for enumerating string union values (route paths, event names) and `readonly` on component properties that should only be set once.
Angular component classes mix instance properties, signal-based state, and constructor-injected services. TypeScript variable declaration rules apply to all of them.
Angular 17+ encourages signals for reactive state. Signals are always declared with const because you call methods on the signal reference — you never replace the signal itself.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Function scope leaks out of blocks; silent re-declaration
TS2588 error — you can't swap the binding
Mutate properties instead, or use let for the binding
ReferenceError in production if code is reorganised
Omit the annotation when the value makes it obvious
type that is consistent with how a variable is declared. With
, literals become their literal type; with
, they widen to the base type. Narrowing is the opposite: TypeScript refines a broad type to something specific inside a conditional branch.
Narrowing works automatically in TypeScript — no casting is required as long as the condition is a standard JavaScript predicate. TypeScript tracks what each branch proves about the type.
) tell TypeScript to treat a value as a specific type. Use them only when you know more about a value than the type system can infer — never use them to silence a legitimate error.
Type assertions bypass type checking at the point of use. If the actual runtime value does not match the asserted type, you get a runtime error, not a compile-time error. Prefer type guards (
Variable Scope in Classes — Angular Perspective
access modifiers to class properties. These are compile-time only — they are erased at runtime.
TypeScript's parameter properties let you declare and assign a class property in one line inside the constructor signature.

Declaring Variables in Different Angular Contexts

directly inside a template. For local template variables, use the
directive (Angular 18+) or a template reference variable (
